news_summary: Innovative approaches to measuring economic health, simple solutions to urban heat, community efforts to protect migratory birds, and a celebration of David Attenborough's legacy highlight today's positive environmental news.
news_summary_fr: Des approches innovantes pour évaluer la santé économique, des solutions simples pour lutter contre la chaleur urbaine, des initiatives communautaires visant à protéger les oiseaux migrateurs et un hommage à l'œuvre de David Attenborough constituent les faits marquants de l'actualité environnementale positive d'aujourd'hui.
news_summary_es: Entre las noticias medioambientales positivas de hoy destacan enfoques innovadores para medir la salud económica, soluciones sencillas al calor urbano, iniciativas comunitarias para proteger a las aves migratorias y un homenaje al legado de David Attenborough.

articles.title: Goodbye GDP? 31 ways to replace the world's favourite measure of economic health
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01511-xProposed UN progress indicators include greenhouse gas emissions, life expectancy and children's performance in reading and maths.
articles.content:
Beyond GDP indicators include measures of air (pictured here in Shanghai).Credit: Jackyenjoyphotography/Getty

The United Nations is considering 31 new indicators to “complement and go beyond” the world’s main measure of economic growth, gross domestic product (GDP).

The proposed new annual indicators include economic metrics such as household disposable income per person, and environmental data such as a country’s greenhouse gas emissions and levels of particulate matter in the air. Also included are health and education indicators such as life expectancy and children’s performance in reading and maths; as well as measures of wellbeing such as the proportion of women and girls subjected to physical and/or sexual violence. Fifteen of the 31 proposed indicators are already part of indicators for the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

The new indicators are in a report, Counting What Counts, written by a multidisciplinary committee of researchers and policymakers, co- chaired by economists Kaushik Basu, based at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York, and Nora Lustig at Tulane University in New Orleans. The group was assembled last year by UN Secretary General António Guterres. Attempts to dethrone GDP, including by the UN, go back at least three decades but this is the first time that a UN chief has responded to such a request from member states. Speaking at a launch event at UN headquarters in New York City on 7 May, Guterres called the report a “landmark step in correcting a longstanding blind spot in measuring progress”.

What GDP is – and isn’t

There are different ways to measure GDP. According to one widely-used method, it is the sum of what a country’s households and governments spend and what businesses invest. The rules for how it is calculated are agreed by the UN Statistical Commission, which is a kind of parliament of the world’s national statistical agencies. But data are compiled and reported by individual countries. It is mentioned in numerous laws and is used as a benchmark in policymaking worldwide. Countries regularly report their defence, health and research spending as a percentage of GDP.

GDP growth is also often the main objective of a country’s economic policy. “It is now being used in ways its architects never intended,” Guterres said. “In my time as secretary-general, the size of the global economy has risen over 50 per cent, adjusting for inflation.” At the same time, many indicators for health, biodiversity, job-creation, human rights, peace “with conflicts now at levels not seen since World War II”, Guterres added, are going backwards.

Annalena Baerbock, UN General Assembly president, added that GDP increases when there are harms. “If many people in your country have accidents on the road or a chemical disaster, also this would at that moment increase your GDP because you have to do something to counter it.”

Mixed signals

The report has drawn mixed reactions from researchers and policymakers.

Rutger Hoekstra, who studies economic, environmental and social indicators at Leiden University in the Netherlands, describes GDP as “a flawed indicator of economic growth”. He adds that it's important to see wellbeing, inclusion and sustainability recognized in the new plan as these “are superior measures of societal progress”.

Paula Caballero, regional director for Latin America at The Nature Conservancy, a conservation non-profit organization based in Arlington, Virginia, adds that the initiative has been a long time in the making. Caballero, a former diplomat from Colombia, was instrumental (with colleague Patti Londoño) in establishing the SDGs .

She says that discussions about moving beyond GDP were part of the SDGs’ negotiations, but that the idea was dropped as there were too many items on the agenda. GDP, she adds is “very short sighted”, as it does not account for the impacts of future growth, such as from climate change.

Stephen Polasky, an environmental economist at the University of Minnesota in Saint Paul, points out that some of the proposed environmental measures, notably biodiversity and natural capital, are limited. “I don't object to anything that is included,” he says, but adds that there should have been more detail on the nature-related indicators. “Inclusion of biodiversity/environmental issues should be a major issue for a report that looks to go beyond GDP. It seems like a missed opportunity.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant UN initiative proposing 31 new indicators to complement and go beyond GDP, incorporating economic, environmental, health, education, and wellbeing metrics. This represents a meaningful shift towards more holistic and sustainable measures of progress with broad global impact, addressing long-standing issues in how economic health is measured and encouraging policies that better reflect societal and environmental wellbeing.
articles.personality_title: UN proposes 31 new ways to measure progress beyond GDP
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, countries have used gross domestic product (GDP) to measure how well their economies are doing. GDP adds up what people, businesses, and governments spend, but it does not show important things like health, education, or the environment.

**What happened** – The United Nations (UN) has suggested 31 new indicators to use alongside GDP. These include measures like how much greenhouse gas a country produces, how long people live, how well children do in reading and math, and how many women face violence. This new list was created by a group of experts and was announced by UN Secretary-General António Guterres on May 7, 2026.

**Impact** – This proposal is important because it shows a new way to understand a country's progress, not just by money but by health, education, safety, and the environment. It responds to problems with GDP, which can go up even when bad things happen, like disasters or pollution. Using these new indicators can help leaders make better decisions that protect people and the planet.

**What's next step** – The UN and its member countries will discuss how to use these new measures. Some experts want even more detailed environmental data added. Over time, these indicators might change how countries report their progress and shape new policies for a healthier world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new set of 31 indicators offers a broader and clearer way to measure a country’s true progress beyond just economic growth.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'ONU propose 31 nouveaux indicateurs pour mesurer le progrès au-delà du PIB
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les pays utilisent le produit intérieur brut (PIB) pour mesurer leur économie. Le PIB additionne les dépenses des personnes, des entreprises et des gouvernements, mais il ne montre pas des choses importantes comme la santé, l'éducation ou l'environnement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L'Organisation des Nations Unies (ONU) a proposé 31 nouveaux indicateurs à utiliser avec le PIB. Ils incluent des mesures comme la quantité de gaz à effet de serre produite, l'espérance de vie, les résultats des enfants en lecture et mathématiques, et le nombre de femmes victimes de violences. Cette liste a été créée par un groupe d'experts et annoncée par le Secrétaire général António Guterres le 7 mai 2026.

**Impact** – Cette proposition est importante car elle offre une nouvelle façon de comprendre le progrès d’un pays, pas seulement avec l'argent mais aussi la santé, l'éducation, la sécurité et l'environnement. Elle répond aux limites du PIB, qui peut augmenter même quand des choses négatives arrivent, comme des catastrophes ou de la pollution. Ces nouveaux indicateurs aideront les dirigeants à prendre de meilleures décisions pour protéger les gens et la planète.

**Étape suivante** – L'ONU et ses membres discuteront de la façon d'utiliser ces nouveaux indicateurs. Certains experts souhaitent ajouter des données environnementales plus détaillées. À l'avenir, ces mesures pourraient changer la manière dont les pays rapportent leurs progrès et guider de nouvelles politiques pour un monde plus sain.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les 31 nouveaux indicateurs de l’ONU offrent une manière plus large et claire de mesurer le vrai progrès d’un pays, au-delà de la seule croissance économique.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU propone 31 nuevas formas de medir el progreso más allá del PIB
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los países han usado el producto interno bruto (PIB) para medir qué tan bien va su economía. El PIB suma lo que gastan las personas, las empresas y los gobiernos, pero no muestra cosas importantes como la salud, la educación o el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Las Naciones Unidas (ONU) han propuesto 31 nuevos indicadores para usar junto con el PIB. Estos incluyen medidas como cuánto gas de efecto invernadero produce un país, la esperanza de vida, el rendimiento de los niños en lectura y matemáticas, y cuántas mujeres sufren violencia. Esta nueva lista fue creada por un grupo de expertos y anunciada por el Secretario General António Guterres el 7 de mayo de 2026.

**Impacto** – Esta propuesta es importante porque muestra una manera nueva de entender el progreso de un país, no solo con dinero sino con salud, educación, seguridad y medio ambiente. Responde a los problemas del PIB, que puede subir incluso cuando ocurren cosas malas, como desastres o contaminación. Usar estos nuevos indicadores puede ayudar a los líderes a tomar mejores decisiones para proteger a las personas y al planeta.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU y sus países miembros discutirán cómo usar estas nuevas medidas. Algunos expertos quieren incluir datos ambientales más detallados. Con el tiempo, estos indicadores podrían cambiar cómo los países informan sobre su progreso y crear nuevas políticas para un mundo más saludable.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo conjunto de 31 indicadores de la ONU ofrece una forma más amplia y clara de medir el verdadero progreso de un país más allá del crecimiento económico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, balanced scale with one side holding a glowing golden GDP coin and the other side filled with diverse symbolic icons representing wellbeing—such as a stylized open book for education, a heart for health, a clean cityscape with clear air and a rising sun for environmental quality, and interconnected hands symbolizing social inclusion—set against a soft, natural color palette with a subtle abstract map of the world in the background.

articles.title: Happy 100th birthday David Attenborough! Nature salutes you
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01473-0The science communicator has raised awareness of the natural world andits myriad interconnections on which humans depend.
articles.content:
It is one of the most memorable moments in natural-history film-making: two young mountain gorillas playing with the broadcaster David Attenborough in Rwanda, later joined by the gorillas’ watchful mother. Attenborough is in his element, quietly demolishing a widely held perception — bolstered over decades by films such as King Kong — that these shy and peaceful creatures pose a violent threat to humans.

The clip, filmed in 1978 for the landmark BBC series Life on Earth: A Natural History, demonstrates what has become Attenborough’s trademark over his 70-year career: the communication of new, surprising and complex phenomena by showing rather than telling. There’s no lecture here — just curiosity, mischief and arresting visual impact.

Attenborough turns 100 on 8 May. Here, Nature salutes him and celebrates his unparalleled impact as a science communicator who has brought nature closer to many millions of people. We also pay tribute in an immersive article that shows his extraordinary life in pictures (see go.nature.com/4wddktw). The inspiration he provides must continue to shape efforts to conserve the natural world.

A scientist and communicator

It’s hard to overstate Attenborough’s influence on science broadcasting. In the early decades of television, programmes fronted by experts usually involved men giving lectures to the camera, with photography and graphics having a secondary role. Attenborough was no less an expert and, unusually for someone at the BBC at the time, had studied science: geology and zoology at the University of Cambridge, UK. But he brought a more involved, engaging approach, getting out into the field. His films are a personal journey of discovery, but the spotlight is shared.

A life in pictures: celebrating David Attenborough at 100

Life on Earth, a 13-part series of hour-long episodes that aired in 1979, told a sweeping story of the origins and evolution of life. It took three years to make, and Attenborough and his crew travelled 2.1 million kilometres to 100 locations in 49 countries, filming 650 species. In letting the cameras (and his now-familiar enthused whisper) do the talking, he took full advantage of the beginning of colour television — an innovation he had been instrumental in bringing to the United Kingdom, during a stint as the controller of the television channel BBC2 in the 1960s. The series was watched by an estimated 500 million people worldwide — more than one-tenth of the global population at the time.

Many of the scenes in Life on Earth had never before been captured on camera, and the series spotlighted not just charismatic megafauna, but also the wonders of life at all scales. One of the most memorable segments involves Darwin’s frogs (Rhinoderma darwinii), a tiny species found in the forests of Chile and Argentina. The males collect and incubate fertilized eggs in their mouths, before dramatically releasing the newly developed froglets.

We are all broadcasters now

Attenborough followed Life on Earth with ever-more-ambitious films on subjects such as mammals, birds and life in the deep ocean, culminating in two further landmark series: Planet Earth and Blue Planet, which he narrated well into his later years. For much of this time, only relatively few well-financed broadcasters had the resources to take on such projects. The BBC’s budget for Life on Earth reached an estimated £1 million (then equivalent to around US$2 million), according to Jean-Baptiste Gouyon, a science-communication researcher at University College London who has studied Attenborough’s documentaries (J.-B. Guoyon BBC Wildlife Documentaries in the Age of Attenborough, 2019). It was an astronomical sum at the time. Today, public-service broadcasters such as the BBC are under considerable financial and political pressure, and so are less likely to take on such risky projects.

Streaming platforms such as Disney+ and Netflix are taking up the baton, satisfying to an extent the market for big-ticket nature documentaries created by Attenborough. But as technology has evolved, so, too, have opportunities to become creators. Camera equipment that was once carted around in trucks and vans now fits in our pockets. Content creators on YouTube or TikTok have the potential to achieve Attenborough levels of viewership — and engagement — regardless of resourcing or where they are in the world.

‘Powerful and horrifying’ — David Attenborough’s latest film is a cry to protect our oceans

There has never been a greater need for such science communication and engagement. Attenborough’s long career has coincided with a period during which humanity’s impact on the planet has vastly increased. His evolving output reflects changing attitudes towards those effects. The first series he presented was Zoo Quest in the 1950s, in which a team from London Zoo collected species from other countries and transported them to London. It would not be commissioned today. Series such as Life on Earth have been criticized for focusing on celebrating the wonders of evolution and the beauty of natural environments, while excluding many people who live in the locations involved, and overlooking threats to such places from unchecked industrial development powered by fossil fuels.

By contrast, Attenborough’s newer films have broadened their focus to include not only species, but also ecosystems — biodiversity in its fullest sense. They have also adopted a more urgent tone about the degradation of the environment; his 2025 film Ocean with David Attenborough, for example, includes heart-wrenching footage of the devastation wrought on ocean-floor ecosystems by the fishing practice of bottom trawling.

And, after many decades of avoiding the lecture format and giving his own opinion, Attenborough has increasingly turned to personal advocacy, warning of the risks of climate change. In a 2021 speech to the United Nations COP26 climate meeting in Glasgow, UK, he told delegates: “In my lifetime I’ve witnessed a terrible decline. In yours, you could and should witness a wonderful recovery.”

Attenborough has arguably done more than anyone else on the planet to raise awareness of the natural world and its myriad interconnections on which humans depend. As we mark his centenary, the most fitting legacy of the next 100 years would be for humanity to achieve that necessary recovery.
articles.reasoning: The article celebrates David Attenborough's 100th birthday and his unparalleled impact as a science communicator who has raised global awareness about the natural world, biodiversity, and climate change. His work has inspired millions worldwide and contributed significantly to conservation efforts and environmental understanding. The story has broad significance, focusing on a single impactful figure and his legacy, with detailed context about his contributions and the ongoing need for environmental recovery.
articles.personality_title: David Attenborough turns 100, celebrating a lifetime of bringing nature closer to millions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – David Attenborough is a famous broadcaster and scientist who has spent over 70 years making films about nature. He helped people understand animals, plants, and how all life on Earth is connected. His work started when TV was still new and has grown with technology.

**What happened** – On May 8, 2026, David Attenborough turned 100 years old. To celebrate, the science journal Nature honored him for his incredible career. One of his most famous moments was in 1978, filming mountain gorillas in Rwanda, showing they are peaceful, not dangerous. His 1979 TV series Life on Earth was watched by half a billion people and showed many animals and plants in color for the first time.

**Impact** – Attenborough changed how nature is shown on TV by going into the wild and showing real moments instead of just talking. His films helped millions of people see the beauty and importance of nature. Over time, he also started to speak more about problems like climate change and ocean damage, encouraging people to protect the planet.

**What's next step** – New technology means anyone can share nature stories now, using phones and the internet. Streaming services like Netflix continue making big nature documentaries inspired by Attenborough’s work. His message about caring for the environment is more important than ever, as humans work to fix the damage done to the Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – David Attenborough’s 100 years of storytelling have helped millions understand and care for the natural world, inspiring ongoing efforts to protect it.
articles.personality_title_fr: David Attenborough fête ses 100 ans, une vie à rapprocher la nature des millions de personnes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – David Attenborough est un célèbre animateur et scientifique qui a passé plus de 70 ans à réaliser des films sur la nature. Il a aidé les gens à comprendre les animaux, les plantes et comment toute la vie sur Terre est reliée. Son travail a commencé à l’époque où la télévision était encore nouvelle et a évolué avec la technologie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 8 mai 2026, David Attenborough a eu 100 ans. Pour célébrer, la revue scientifique Nature lui a rendu hommage pour sa carrière exceptionnelle. Un de ses moments les plus célèbres date de 1978, lorsqu’il a filmé des gorilles de montagne au Rwanda, montrant qu’ils sont paisibles, pas dangereux. Sa série télévisée Life on Earth de 1979 a été vue par un demi-milliard de personnes et a montré de nombreux animaux et plantes en couleur pour la première fois.

**Impact** – Attenborough a changé la façon de montrer la nature à la télévision en allant sur le terrain et en montrant des moments vrais plutôt que simplement parler. Ses films ont aidé des millions de personnes à voir la beauté et l’importance de la nature. Au fil du temps, il a aussi commencé à parler davantage des problèmes comme le changement climatique et la destruction des océans, encourageant à protéger la planète.

**Prochaine étape** – La nouvelle technologie permet à chacun de partager des histoires sur la nature, grâce aux téléphones et à Internet. Des plateformes comme Netflix continuent de produire de grands documentaires sur la nature, inspirés par le travail d’Attenborough. Son message sur la protection de l’environnement est plus important que jamais, alors que l’humanité tente de réparer les dégâts causés à la Terre.

**Phrase clé** – Les 100 ans de récits de David Attenborough ont aidé des millions de personnes à comprendre et à prendre soin du monde naturel, inspirant des efforts continus pour le protéger.
articles.personality_title_es: David Attenborough cumple 100 años, celebrando una vida acercando la naturaleza a millones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – David Attenborough es un conocido presentador y científico que ha pasado más de 70 años haciendo películas sobre la naturaleza. Ha ayudado a las personas a entender animales, plantas y cómo toda la vida en la Tierra está conectada. Su trabajo comenzó cuando la televisión era nueva y ha crecido con la tecnología.

**Qué pasó** – El 8 de mayo de 2026, David Attenborough cumplió 100 años. Para celebrarlo, la revista científica Nature lo honró por su increíble carrera. Uno de sus momentos más famosos fue en 1978, filmando gorilas de montaña en Ruanda, mostrando que son pacíficos, no peligrosos. Su serie de televisión Life on Earth de 1979 fue vista por quinientos millones de personas y mostró muchos animales y plantas en color por primera vez.

**Impacto** – Attenborough cambió la forma de mostrar la naturaleza en la televisión al salir al campo y mostrar momentos reales en lugar de solo hablar. Sus películas ayudaron a millones a ver la belleza y la importancia de la naturaleza. Con el tiempo, también comenzó a hablar más sobre problemas como el cambio climático y el daño a los océanos, animando a proteger el planeta.

**Próximo paso** – La nueva tecnología permite que cualquiera pueda compartir historias de la naturaleza usando teléfonos y el internet. Plataformas como Netflix continúan produciendo grandes documentales de naturaleza inspirados en el trabajo de Attenborough. Su mensaje sobre cuidar el medio ambiente es más importante que nunca, mientras la humanidad trabaja para reparar el daño hecho a la Tierra.

**Frase clave** – Los 100 años de historias de David Attenborough han ayudado a millones a entender y cuidar el mundo natural, inspirando esfuerzos continuos para protegerlo.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a pair of playful young mountain gorillas interacting with a symbolic glowing silhouette of a wise, aged figure formed from soft light and natural textures, set against the lush green forests of Rwanda at dawn, evoking curiosity, harmony, and a century of nature storytelling.

articles.title: The solution to urban heat is much, much simpler than you think
articles.content:
Johnny Appleseed was ahead of his time. Not because he fed so many people by planting apple trees (really, he got them drunk instead, as his real goal was encouraging the production of cider), but because he created so much shade to enjoy on hot days. More than two centuries later, American cities are wishing they had better followed Appleseed’s lead, as rising temperatures and a lack of tree cover combine to make urban life increasingly stifling.

Two new studies show how simply planting more trees can provide huge temperature benefits, not to mention how the additional plant life would boost biodiversity and improve mental health for urbanites. The first finds that tree cover can cancel half of the heat island effect, in which the urban jungle gets much hotter than the surrounding countryside. The second compares neighborhoods in 65 American cities, finding that canopy-deprived areas suffer up to 40 percent more excess heat than heavily greened spots.

Places like New York and Atlanta and Los Angeles, then, don’t just have to foster and maintain their “gray” infrastructure — roads and sidewalks and such — but their living infrastructure as well. “Heat is already a major public health threat. It kills 350,000 people a year by some estimates, and it’s worse in cities,” said Robert McDonald, the Nature Conservancy’s lead scientist for nature-based solutions and the Europe region, who spearheaded the first paper. “The urban heat island effect would be about double what it is now if world cities didn’t have trees.”

By increasing their canopies, metropolises dress themselves like their more comfortable rural counterparts. A vegetated area cools itself both because plants “sweat” by releasing moisture from their leaves, and because trees provide shade. By contrast, concrete absorbs the sun’s energy, driving temperatures up, and releases it throughout the night. That beats back the cooling typically experienced in the evening, meaning urbanites without air conditioning don’t get respite. This is especially dangerous for vulnerable groups like the elderly, and it’s one reason heat kills more Americans every year than all other extreme weather events combined.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Such conditions are especially dangerous for those living in lower-income neighborhoods, which tend to have significantly less tree canopy than richer areas. In industrialized areas, for example, vast stretches of concrete absorb and radiate heat. In urban centers, policymakers may have prioritized building dense housing without incorporating ample tree cover. Compare that to the suburbs, which have plenty of parks, curbside trees, and yards to cool things down.

The differences in greenery between neighborhoods translates into striking differences in temperatures. The second study calculated this “cooling dividend,” or the difference in the average urban heat island in areas with low and high canopy cover. It found gaps reaching almost 4 degrees Fahrenheit. If you’re lucky enough to live where there’s lots of trees, you might experience 20 to 40 percent less excess heat. The report found that this is playing out regularly across the U.S. “I think what maybe was surprising is that there was a dramatic amount of consistency,” said Steve Whitesell, executive editor at the Healthy Green Spaces Coalition, which authored the report. “In other words, they were all showing an impact.”

The trick is not just planting enough trees, but planting the right kind. The biggest species provide the most shade, of course. But more cryptically, some provide more evaporative cooling than others — drought-adapted trees, for instance, try to retain as much water as they can. A neighborhood might also want to prioritize food production, opting for trees that create both shade and fruit. Favoring native varieties will also help support native animal life, like birds and pollinating insects.

Climate change, though, is complicating these calculations. Even in rural areas, without the added temperatures of the urban heat island effect, some places are getting so hot that native plants are moving north in search of cooler climes. Within cities, they are blasted with still more heat — and temperatures will only climb from here. So urban arborists aren’t just planting species that will thrive today, but will survive the climate of tomorrow. “I think that for us to use trees as a type of living infrastructure, that can counter those increased temperatures, is paramount,” said Edith de Guzman, a cooperative extension researcher at the University of California, Los Angeles, who studies urban heat but wasn’t involved in either study. “I think it’s pretty much the most important thing we can do.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

But trees alone can’t save urbanites. McDonald’s study found that even if cities planted as many as possible, it would only offset 20 percent of the potential running up of temperatures due to climate change. Designers will have to deploy other techniques, like reflective rooftops, to manage the heat. That’s especially important in poorer nations, whose cities are rapidly growing but have much less tree cover than richer countries, the study found. “It’s just to say that climate change is a big enough challenge that while planting more tree cover helps with temperatures, it won’t do the job by itself,” McDonald said.

Urban areas have been here before, McDonald added. As the Industrial Revolution kicked in, people in overpopulated metropolises would have to travel to the countryside to glimpse greenery. An exception was London, with its many publicly available green spaces, which Paris took as inspiration when it essentially rebuilt itself in the 1800s and made room for massive parks. Today, planners are similarly bringing some of the country back into the city, blurring the lines between rural and urban. “We know how to increase tree cover, if we put our minds to it,” McDonald said. “But it takes effort and time.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on two new studies demonstrating that increasing urban tree cover can significantly reduce the urban heat island effect, providing broad public health and environmental benefits. It highlights the measurable impact of tree planting on temperature reduction, biodiversity, and mental health, with implications for millions of city dwellers. The article provides detailed context on the problem, the studies' findings, and practical considerations for implementation, making it a substantive and inspiring environmental solution.
articles.personality_title: Planting trees can cool cities and improve life, studies show
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cities are getting hotter because of many buildings and little shade. This problem, called the 'urban heat island effect,' means cities become much warmer than nearby countryside. It makes summer days harder, especially for people without air conditioning.

**What happened** – Two new studies in the United States found that planting more trees in cities can lower temperatures a lot. One study showed trees can cut the extra heat in half. The other compared neighborhoods in 65 cities and found that places with fewer trees experienced up to 40% more heat than greener areas. Trees cool by giving shade and releasing moisture, while concrete and roads absorb and keep heat.

**Impact** – These findings are important because they show a simple way to make cities cooler and healthier. More trees not only reduce dangerous heat but also help animals and improve people's mental health. The studies also highlight that poorer neighborhoods often have fewer trees and face more heat, which can affect people’s health.

**What's next step** – City planners need to plant more trees, choosing types that grow big and survive future hotter climates. But trees alone won’t solve all heat problems caused by climate change. Other solutions, like reflective roofs, will also be needed. Still, increasing tree cover is a key step to make cities more comfortable and safe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Planting more and better trees in cities can greatly reduce dangerous heat and improve life for millions of people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Planter des arbres peut rafraîchir les villes et améliorer la vie, montrent les études
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les villes deviennent plus chaudes à cause des nombreux bâtiments et du manque d’ombre. Ce phénomène, appelé « îlot de chaleur urbain », rend les villes beaucoup plus chaudes que la campagne proche. Cela rend les journées d’été plus difficiles, surtout pour les personnes sans climatisation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Deux nouvelles études aux États-Unis ont montré que planter plus d’arbres dans les villes peut beaucoup faire baisser la température. Une étude a montré que les arbres peuvent réduire la chaleur supplémentaire de moitié. L’autre a comparé des quartiers dans 65 villes et a trouvé que les endroits avec moins d’arbres avaient jusqu’à 40 % de chaleur en plus que les zones plus vertes. Les arbres rafraîchissent en faisant de l’ombre et en libérant de l’humidité, tandis que le béton et les routes absorbent et gardent la chaleur.

**Impact** – Ces résultats sont importants car ils montrent un moyen simple de rendre les villes plus fraîches et plus saines. Plus d’arbres réduisent non seulement la chaleur dangereuse, mais aident aussi les animaux et améliorent la santé mentale des habitants. Les études soulignent aussi que les quartiers pauvres ont souvent moins d’arbres et subissent plus la chaleur, ce qui peut nuire à la santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les responsables des villes doivent planter plus d’arbres, en choisissant des espèces qui poussent grandes et qui résisteront aux climats plus chauds à venir. Mais les arbres seuls ne résoudront pas tous les problèmes de chaleur causés par le changement climatique. D’autres solutions, comme des toits réfléchissants, seront aussi nécessaires. Pourtant, augmenter la couverture d’arbres est une étape clé pour rendre les villes plus agréables et sûres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Planter plus et mieux d’arbres en ville peut fortement réduire la chaleur dangereuse et améliorer la vie de millions de personnes.
articles.personality_title_es: Plantar árboles puede enfriar las ciudades y mejorar la vida, muestran estudios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las ciudades se están volviendo más calientes debido a muchos edificios y poca sombra. Este problema, llamado 'efecto isla de calor urbano', hace que las ciudades sean mucho más cálidas que el campo cercano. Esto dificulta los días de verano, especialmente para quienes no tienen aire acondicionado.

**Qué pasó** – Dos nuevos estudios en Estados Unidos encontraron que plantar más árboles en las ciudades puede bajar mucho las temperaturas. Un estudio mostró que los árboles pueden reducir la temperatura extra a la mitad. El otro comparó barrios en 65 ciudades y encontró que los lugares con menos árboles tenían hasta un 40 % más de calor que las zonas con más árboles. Los árboles enfrían al dar sombra y liberar humedad, mientras que el concreto y las calles absorben y retienen el calor.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos son importantes porque muestran una forma sencilla de hacer las ciudades más frescas y saludables. Más árboles no solo reducen el calor peligroso, sino que también ayudan a los animales y mejoran la salud mental de las personas. Los estudios también destacan que los barrios más pobres suelen tener menos árboles y sufren más calor, lo que puede afectar la salud.

**Próximo paso** – Los planificadores urbanos deben plantar más árboles, eligiendo tipos que crezcan grandes y sobrevivan a climas más cálidos en el futuro. Pero los árboles por sí solos no solucionarán todos los problemas de calor causados por el cambio climático. También se necesitarán otras soluciones, como techos reflectantes. Aun así, aumentar la cobertura de árboles es un paso clave para hacer las ciudades más cómodas y seguras.

**Resumen en una frase** – Plantar más y mejores árboles en las ciudades puede reducir mucho el calor peligroso y mejorar la vida de millones de personas.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant cityscape blending seamlessly into a lush orchard of large apple trees whose expansive canopies cast cool, dappled shade over sunlit streets and diverse neighborhoods, with gentle wisps of mist rising from leaves symbolizing natural evaporative cooling, all rendered in soft, earthy tones and warm light.

articles.title: ‘Every Bird Counts’: How city residents can help protect migratory birds
articles.summary: Did you know that millions of birds may be migrating silently overhead right now as you read this – and you don’t even need to leave the house to see them?
articles.content:
Ahead of World Migratory Bird Day on 9 May, UN News spoke to Catherine Quayle, Communications Director at the Wild Bird Fund in New York.

This year’s theme, Every Bird Counts, Your Observations Matter, highlights the urgent need to protect migratory birds and the ecosystems they depend on.

UN News: For people in cities, it can feel like wildlife is far away – how are migratory birds still part of our daily lives?

Catherine Quayle: If you live anywhere with trees and a bit of green, even in the middle of Manhattan, you can hear birds when you wake up. Their songs change over the year. You don't even need to leave home to witness the ecological diversity outside your window.

Birds also often migrate at night. In the evening, you might see gulls or herons migrating to roost. Look up early in the morning or in the evening, and you'll see birds without leaving home.

Wild Bird Fund

UN News: New York City sits on the Atlantic Flyway; basically a bird highway. How many birds pass over our heads during peak migration?

Catherine Quayle: BirdCast, a tool from the Cornell Lab of Ornithology, uses weather radar to track bird migration, showing how many are flying and when.

On peak nights, a few billion birds may be in the air over the US at once. Over New York City, as many as a million can pass in a single night, but most people are unaware because it happens at night.

UN News: What are some of the challenges birds face when they migrate?

Catherine Quayle: One of the biggest challenges is habitat loss. Birds migrate to find specific food available at certain times of year. For generations, they have learned where to go, for example, Central America in winter.

But if that habitat has been taken down, built over, paved, or logged, many more birds will not survive when they arrive at their wintering or breeding grounds.

The other major threat in New York City is window collisions. This is likely much worse than 50 years ago because modern architecture is very glass-centric. Highly glazed surfaces and all-glass windows are very popular, and that is just very dangerous for birds.

Wild Bird Fund/Terria Clay

UN News: Are there certain types of buildings or locations more dangerous for birds?

Catherine Quayle: Skyscrapers are dangerous because their bright lights attract birds, drawing them into areas like downtown Manhattan during migration, where glass surfaces reflect nearby trees. The combination of light and glass is highly hazardous.

But homes can be just as risky. Reflections, especially near feeders or trees, often lead to collisions. Homes actually cause the majority of collisions, they're just less noticeable because it's one or two birds at a time, whereas in downtown Manhattan you might find a hundred.

Wild Bird Fund/Rachel Frank

Migratory birds are especially at risk because they arrive at night, tired, disoriented, and in unfamiliar territory.

UN News: What can we do on a daily basis to contribute to bird conservation?

Catherine Quayle: You can keep your outdoor lights off at night so the skies stay darker and birds aren’t attracted to the light. In a smaller community, people turning out their lights makes a big difference.

One of the best things you can do is plant native species in your garden. Do a little research on what species are native to your area and devote some of your outdoor space to native species to attract insects and birds.

UN News: Do you have a memorable rescue story that really stuck with you?

Catherine Quayle: When I visit a nearby park to watch birds, I pass several buildings where window collisions are common. During migration season, I check for injured birds.

On one trip, I spotted a bird in the road beneath a glass skybridge – structures like these are especially dangerous because birds don’t expect them and often collide with the windows.

I ran down the hill and scooped the bird up; seconds later, a car drove over the spot where it had been. The bird was a scarlet tanager in beautiful yellow plumage. It was later rehabilitated and released, and it would certainly have been hit if I hadn’t picked it up.

© Wikimedia/Félix Uribe

UN News: For people who may not think of themselves as bird people, what’s one small thing they can do?

Catherine Quayle: Start by noticing the birds around you, what you see and hear when you go outside. As you pay attention, your curiosity will grow. You might pick up binoculars or join a bird walk.

During migration, many free bird walks are offered in local parks, often led by experts, and they’re really fun.

The first time I walked into Central Park with a pair of binoculars many years ago and started looking at all the birds I hadn't noticed before, it blew my mind. I was so excited and couldn't get enough of it. I've been doing it ever since, and that was 25 years ago.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful environmental conservation effort focused on protecting migratory birds, emphasizing broad ecological benefits and actionable steps for the general public. It provides detailed context on the challenges birds face, the scale of migration, and practical ways individuals can contribute to bird conservation, making it an inspiring and informative story with significant real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: How city residents can help protect millions of migratory birds overhead
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, millions of birds travel long distances during migration, often flying over cities at night. Many face dangers like loss of habitat and collisions with buildings, especially glass windows.

**What happened** – Ahead of World Migratory Bird Day on May 9, experts from the Wild Bird Fund in New York shared how city residents can notice and help protect these birds. Using tools like BirdCast, scientists track billions of birds flying over places like New York City each night during peak migration.

**Impact** – This story shows that even in big cities, birds are a part of our daily lives, often unseen. It explains how modern buildings with glass and bright lights can accidentally harm birds. It also highlights simple actions people can take, such as turning off outdoor lights at night and planting native plants, to make cities safer for migrating birds.

**What's next step** – People are encouraged to observe birds around them and join local bird walks during migration seasons. This growing awareness can lead to more community efforts to protect birds and improve urban habitats.

**One-sentence takeaway** – City residents can play a vital role in protecting migratory birds by making small changes like reducing light pollution and planting native species near their homes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les citadins peuvent aider à protéger des millions d’oiseaux migrateurs au-dessus de leur tête
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, des millions d’oiseaux migrent sur de longues distances, souvent au-dessus des villes pendant la nuit. Beaucoup sont en danger à cause de la perte d’habitat et des collisions avec les fenêtres en verre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Avant la Journée mondiale des oiseaux migrateurs le 9 mai, des experts du Wild Bird Fund à New York ont expliqué comment les citadins peuvent observer et protéger ces oiseaux. Avec des outils comme BirdCast, les scientifiques suivent des milliards d’oiseaux qui passent au-dessus de villes comme New York pendant les pics de migration.

**Impact** – Cette histoire montre que même dans les grandes villes, les oiseaux font partie de notre quotidien, souvent invisibles. Elle explique comment les bâtiments modernes avec des surfaces vitrées et des lumières vives peuvent nuire aux oiseaux. Elle souligne aussi des gestes simples que chacun peut faire, comme éteindre les lumières extérieures la nuit et planter des espèces locales, pour rendre les villes plus sûres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les habitants sont invités à observer les oiseaux autour d’eux et à participer aux balades ornithologiques locales pendant les saisons de migration. Cette prise de conscience peut renforcer les efforts communautaires pour protéger les oiseaux et améliorer les habitats urbains.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les citadins peuvent aider à protéger les oiseaux migrateurs en réduisant la pollution lumineuse et en plantant des espèces locales près de chez eux.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los habitantes de la ciudad pueden ayudar a proteger a millones de aves migratorias sobre sus cabezas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, millones de aves migran largas distancias, a menudo volando sobre las ciudades durante la noche. Muchas enfrentan peligros como la pérdida de hábitat y choques con ventanas de vidrio.

**Qué pasó** – Antes del Día Mundial de las Aves Migratorias, el 9 de mayo, expertos del Wild Bird Fund en Nueva York explicaron cómo los residentes urbanos pueden observar y ayudar a proteger estas aves. Usando herramientas como BirdCast, los científicos rastrean miles de millones de aves que pasan sobre ciudades como Nueva York durante las noches de mayor migración.

**Impacto** – La historia muestra que las aves forman parte de la vida diaria en la ciudad, aunque no siempre las veamos. Explica cómo los edificios modernos con mucho vidrio y luces brillantes pueden ser peligrosos para las aves. También destaca acciones sencillas que las personas pueden hacer, como apagar las luces exteriores por la noche y plantar especies nativas, para hacer las ciudades más seguras.

**Próximo paso** – Se anima a las personas a observar las aves a su alrededor y unirse a paseos de observación de aves en parques locales durante la temporada de migración. Este interés creciente puede impulsar más esfuerzos comunitarios para proteger a las aves y mejorar los hábitats urbanos.

**Frase clave** – Los habitantes de la ciudad pueden ayudar a proteger a las aves migratorias con pequeños cambios como reducir la contaminación lumínica y plantar especies nativas cerca de sus hogares.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle nighttime cityscape with softly glowing skyscraper silhouettes whose windows reflect delicate, colorful migratory birds in mid-flight, while a nearby quiet garden with native plants shelters attentive bird silhouettes listening to bird songs, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones with a detailed, painterly style.
